それでCODEX使ってコードの確認をする事にしようとしたら、その後、半日で急に処理が全画像で終わった…
たまたまとは思うんだけど、
ちょっと安心して全任せは私はできない。
私の設定の仕方がよくないんだろうけど。

昨日からClaude Codeの課金体制変わって60万円請求くるという話もみかけたので、フル任せは怖い😱
Notionのオートメーションの書き方やClaude Codeのルーチンの作り方研究したり最適なプロンプト探っていったりするの楽しい

quoted_status.full_text:
You don't need a $500 course to set up Claude Code. You need 30 minutes.  

Here's the exact sequence ⬇️

1. Install — use the native installer 
(Copy installation command from https://t.co/neWBSOsEVY and run in your terminal or view image below) 

2. Authenticate — run `claude` and log in with your Anthropic account. No API key juggling. 

 3. Write CLAUDE.md — drop a file in your project root telling Claude how your codebase works, or run `/init` so Claude scans your repo and writes a starter file for you.  

4. Run your first task — describe what you want in plain English, not code. Let it read the repo first.  

5. Add one hook — A hook is a script that runs automatically before or after a tool call.

To test, ask Claude "add a hook that runs my formatter after every edit." It writes it into .claude/settings.json for you.  

6. Set permissions — run `/permissions` and allow the commands you trust to run without asking. Stop clicking "yes" 40 times an hour.  

That's the whole setup.  

🔖Save this for when you start your next project.  

#ClaudeCode #AI #Productivity #LearnAI

tweets.full_text:
Last night I ran ichat on termi with strix-claude-code running in the background

First thing I did was ask them to introduce themselves to each other. Then asked them to start hunting for dependency confusion on all the targets they can access through the MCP configured in strix-claude-code

They started talking to each other, fetching JS files from in scope assets, checking package names against npm. Ran for around an hour like this

Found 4 dependency confusions. 3 on h1 and 1 on intigriti

But the part that I did not expect was what ichat did after finding them. It went ahead and opened the H1 report assistant and started drafting the report. It added all the details and then asked the H1 report assistant to write the title, set the severity and pick the weakness category

I was just watching two AIs talking to each other, hunting for bugs, and then one of them filing the report using another AI inside the platform. Did not expect to see that when I started this

ichat running on DeepSeek V4 Pro and strix-claude-code on Opus 4.8 xhigh

user.name: Novemh

tweets.full_text:
New post on https://t.co/j5UaKGaEf3!

Treating autonomous agents as untrusted participants: what the Claude Code harness suggests for on-chain mechanism design

By:
- WGlynn

🔗 https://t.co/JQNqxUf6dQ

Highlights:
- Most real-world “agent” systems rely far more on constraint/guardrail infrastructure than on the model itself (permissioning, isolation, context management, approvals).
- The harness architecture maps cleanly to mechanism design: default-deny permissions act like clearing rules, isolation prevents cross-agent interference, and approval checkpoints function as commitment devices.
- To narrow the on-chain/off-chain enforcement gap, protocols shouldn’t depend on participant honesty; they should change incentives and constraints so defection is unprofitable ("augmenting the invariant").
- As autonomous agents become major on-chain participants, modeling them as perfectly rational and well-specified may be wrong; mechanisms may need to account for fallibility (agents sometimes take non-best-response actions with meaningful probability).
- A key open design question is how to align enforcement domains: can an agent’s off-chain permission envelope be committed/enforced on-chain, and what can mechanism design teach agent-harness engineering (and vice versa) about building robust constraints around untrusted actors?

ELI5:
Imagine you have a very smart robot helper, but sometimes it confidently does the wrong thing. Instead of hoping it behaves, you put it in a playpen with rules: it can’t touch dangerous buttons, it has to ask before doing big actions, and it can’t mess with other robots. The article says blockchain mechanisms should treat AI agents the same way: assume they can be wrong or selfish, and design the system so bad actions don’t pay and are hard or impossible to execute.

tweets.full_text:
I built a "personal 24/7 AI-led and AI-generated radio station." 

Awesome post from Reddit on a DJ who built a fully autonomous, personal 24/7 radio station, led and generated entirely by AI. 

It produces its own music, commercials, ads, shows, hosts, and schedule, then runs the whole operation on autopilot.

He feeds it news stories, world events, and live data from a smart home and sensors and then lets it handle the rest. 

Claude Code stays on as the station IT tech, picking up any upgrades, fixes, or changes whenever something needs updating.

Some of the coolest parts of the build:

- Streams over Icecast to his own domain, with a web app vibe coded for playback.

- Built an Alexa skill so it plays through multiple devices around the house whenever he wants.

- The free tier of Gemini and OpenRouter writes all the scripting for shows, segments, and ad reads.

- Text-to-speech runs locally through Kokoro, so every host has its own voice.

- Suno generates all the music

On whether it costs a lot of tokens: Just "my existing Claude code subscription to run any upgrades, fixes, etc. when needed."

You can just build things.

WHILE YOU’RE RE-EXPLAINING YOUR STACK FOR THE 10TH TIME THIS WEEK…

A small group of builders set up Hermes Agent on a $5 server.

They wake up to finished work.

Here’s what most people are missing:

Claude Code, Cursor, Codex — all session-based.

You open. You explain. You close. Next day: start from zero.

Every. Single. Morning.

Hermes is the opposite.

It lives on your server 24/7.
It remembers every conversation.
It writes its own skill files after every task.
It reaches you on Telegram while you sleep.

The longer you run it — the less you explain.

The setup:

🧠 SOUL.md = agent identity (replaces CLAUDE.md)
📱 Telegram in 5 min via BotFather
🗂️ Profiles = your AI org chart
⚙️ /goal command = overnight autonomous runs

Real example:
“Research top 5 AI design tools, draft comparison post.”
Set max_turns to 30. Go to sleep.
Wake up to a finished draft.

Claude Code wins focused coding.
Hermes wins everything around it.

Most builders are only running half the stack.

The ones who set up both this year will look unfairly ahead in 2026.

Bookmark this. Run it this week.
